summary: O'Neil questions the assumption that big businesses are inherently better, exploring whether small companies might actually be more ethical. She cites examples of large-scale corporate corruption like HSBC's money laundering and JPMorgan's London Whale scandal, contrasting these with small email providers who shut down rather than compromise customer privacy. While initially suggesting small companies have more ethical accountability due to direct customer relationships, she updates her thesis to acknowledge the complexity: small companies can also exploit their size to avoid scrutiny, and are often used as political cover for bad policies.
key_points: Large corporations benefit from 'too big to jail' protection, escaping serious consequences for major crimes like money laundering and fraud
Small companies may have stronger ethical incentives because they depend on direct customer trust and can't afford PR cleanup
The small email providers Lavabit and Silent Circle chose to shut down rather than compromise customer data—something unimaginable for Facebook or Google
Small companies can also be problematic: they may lack unionization, exploit their size to avoid accountability, and serve as political cover for bad policies
money_quotes: When asked why nobody's gotten into trouble, his answer was simple: too big to jail.
Can anyone imagine Facebook or Google doing that? The big business version of this is 'hiring really fancy lawyers' I guess, but it doesn't seem to work as well.
In general, can we claim that small companies have less to lose and therefore have more ethics?
Small companies live and die based on the relationship of trust that they have with their customers.
Larger firms, on the other hand, can hire PR firms to fix their image after the fact if things go wrong.
Small companies having less to lose can also be a super bad thing, if you want to get away with bad shit.
Small companies are often inefficient (so: no unionization) and are used as a political baby seal to justify all sorts of crappy policies.
key_insight: The relationship between company size and ethics is more complex than simple 'small is virtuous' narratives suggest—scale creates different accountability structures rather than inherently more or less ethical behavior.
